project_overview.project_name: Enhancing Community Resilience and Local Governance Project Phase II (ECRP-II)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve access to services, strengthen flood resilience, and enhance institutional capacity for local service delivery and integrated disaster risk management at the national and sub-national levels.
project_overview.beneficiaries: Over 920,000 people across 12 vulnerable counties in South Sudan, including IDPs, refugees, and host communities (approximately 50% women)

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - South Sudan is classified as a fragile, conflict, and violence (FCV) affected state with widespread inter-communal violence, displacement, and insecurity. The project specifically targets conflict-affected and flood-prone vulnerable counties.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Infrastructure and Services for Community Resilience
key_components.description: Supports community-level infrastructure and services through participatory planning including water supply, sanitation, footpaths, community roads, dykes for flood protection, health and education facilities. Includes subcomponent for flood risk reduction investments (levees, dykes, drainage) and operations and maintenance support.

Strengthens community institutions (BDCs/PDCs), county governments, and national/state government capacity for local service delivery and disaster risk management. Includes training, performance-based grants to counties, and support for intergovernmental fiscal transfers.

Supports project management, monitoring and evaluation, third-party monitoring, impact evaluation, and grievance redress mechanism. Includes PMU operating costs and county government performance grants.
Allows rapid reallocation of uncommitted funds in response to eligible emergencies (natural or man-made disasters). Currently allocated US$0 but can be activated as needed.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Finance and Planning (MoFP) in collaboration with Local Government Board (LGB). International Organization for Migration (IOM) contracted as implementing partner for Components 1 and 2 activities.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Steering Committee (NSC) for policy oversight; National Technical Working Group (NTWG) for technical issues; State Coordination Teams (SCTs); County Coordination Teams (CCTs); coordination platform with development partners (UNHCR, GIZ, World Bank, IOM)
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations; use of Standard Procurement Documents; prior and post review thresholds; STEP system for tracking; direct contracting of IOM through Output Agreement; hiring of experienced procurement and FM consultants to support PMU
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Geo-enabled monitoring system with smartphone-based tracking; Third-Party Monitoring Agent (TPMA) for quarterly monitoring; iterative conflict assessments; beneficiary feedback and grievance redress mechanism; impact evaluation; just-in-time studies; quarterly interim financial reports; internal audit by Internal Audit Directorate; annual external audit by National Audit Chamber

identified_risks.risk_description: Complex FCV environment where external interventions can inadvertently create harm; injection of resources may have political economy implications; risk of elite capture; tensions between government and opposition entities; potential for project resources to become targets of manipulation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Transparent and inclusive planning process; fostering social cohesion through participatory planning; investing in public goods benefiting entire communities; design flexibility for adjustments during implementation; conflict analysts on project team; conflict-sensitive implementation approach; monitoring for early risk identification

identified_risks.risk_description: High inflation, currency devaluation, and exchange rate volatility affecting project budget allocations; oil price volatility; limited access to concessional financing; budget process weaknesses; debt transparency issues
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Contracts with local contractors denominated in US dollars; direct disbursements to IOM based on signed contracts; limited local currency payments; engagement with Government on PFM and economic management reforms

identified_risks.risk_description: Significant capacity gaps in Government; weak institutional capacity at national and sub-national levels; high staff attrition and absenteeism in local governments; lack of regular fiscal transfers to counties; weak governance and accountability systems; limited technical capacity for project implementation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Engagement of IOM as implementing partner for decentralized activities; hiring of experienced technical consultants for PMU; capacity building for PMU, county governments, and national institutions; on-the-job training and knowledge transfer; performance-based grants to incentivize county government engagement; support for intergovernmental fiscal transfers

identified_risks.risk_description: Access restrictions limiting supervision; weak institutional capacity; weak governance and accountability systems; rising inflation and currency weakness; challenges in ensuring accountability at county level; difficulty in effective project supervision due to COVID-19, natural hazards, and insecurity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IOM implementation of core components with fiduciary expertise; hiring of experienced FM and procurement consultants; Third-Party Monitoring Agent engagement; geo-enabled monitoring system; regular internal and external audits; IOM's Office of Inspector General annual financial audit; robust monitoring systems including TPMA and conflict assessments

identified_risks.risk_description: High levels of violence and insecurity; inter-communal violence; potential for project assets to become targets; difficulty accessing project sites; volatile security context; potential for suspension of activities in insecure areas
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Development of implementation strategy for insecure areas with objective criteria for classifying insecurity levels; suspension of activities if IOM cannot access areas; option to replace counties if security deteriorates; documented weekly security meetings; tracking of security alert status; site-specific emergency response protocols; integration with UN security provisions (UNDSS); non-militarized approach to local engagement; trust building with communities

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential adverse E&S impacts from construction/rehabilitation of infrastructure; risks of driving existing tensions and conflicts; tensions between communities over project implementation; potential for elite capture; GBV risks; risks related to forced migration and displacement; tensions between refugees and hosts; potential for project to become subject to political manipulation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) with negative list of excluded interventions; E&S screening process for all subprojects; special E&S screening for flood risk reduction investments; inclusive and transparent planning process; geographic targeting guided by vulnerability framework; subproject budgets allocated by formula; public goods focus to promote equity; inclusive community institutions; participatory prioritization processes; robust GRM with survivor-centered protocols; social audits; ongoing conflict monitoring; gender transformative training; GBV Action Plan; training on GBV prevention and referral pathways

identified_risks.risk_description: South Sudan highly prone to hydrometeorological hazards; recurrent droughts and floods; succession of severe flooding (2019, 2020, 2021) causing extensive damage; changing rainfall patterns due to climate variability; inaccessibility of target areas even during dry season; extensive damage to community infrastructure from floods
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Investments in flood risk reduction infrastructure; institutional strengthening for DRM; climate and disaster-resilient design of community infrastructure; safe siting and elevated building structures; inclusion of replacement counties and budget allocation flexibility; longer project period for contingency; phased implementation with disaster risk mapping; iterative conflict monitoring and M&E; geo-spatially enabled remote monitoring; regular field assessments

identified_risks.risk_description: High prevalence of GBV in South Sudan (65% of women and girls report lifetime experience of physical or sexual assault); increased exposure due to conflict, displacement, and insecurity; risks at labor sites; potential for retaliation against survivors raising grievances
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Participatory identification of GBV risks at subproject level with women and girls; safety planning tools; comprehensive safeguards from SEA and coercion at labor sites; public works code of conduct; routine safety audits; GRM with survivor-centered protocols and appeals committees; confidentiality and non-discrimination measures; GBV service mapping and referral pathways; referral pathway training for project personnel; psychological first aid training; recurring gender/GBV audits; engagement of gender and GBV specialists; GBV Action Plan

identified_risks.risk_description: While South Sudan has progressive refugee framework, implementation hindered by capacity limitations of Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA) and other government institutions; challenging socioeconomic environment; potential tensions between refugees and host communities
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: World Bank confirmation of adequacy of South Sudan's refugee protection framework in consultation with UNHCR; support for Government capacity building through project activities; policy dialogue on refugee issues; engagement with UNHCR for guidance on refugee inclusion; equitable benefit allocation to both refugees and host communities; support for Government's refugee strategy implementation; monitoring of protection framework adequacy

identified_risks.risk_description: COVID-19 impacts including movement restrictions, inaccessibility, supply chain disruptions, and increased material costs; recurring floods causing access constraints; youth unrest; O&M challenges; weak informal institutions due to conflict; low trust in government institutions
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Longer project period (5 years) allowing adequate contingency; inception phase to revisit rollout plan based on ground conditions; phased implementation with in-depth assessments; iterative conflict monitoring and stronger M&E; flexibility in budget allocation; cash-for-work modalities; youth engagement in construction and capacity building; context-specific O&M approaches; hybrid O&M models; performance-based grants to incentivize behavioral change; continuous learning by doing approach

identified_risks.risk_description: Entrenched political division at national level; persistence of elevated violence and conflict dynamics; potential for forced displacement if violence increases; tensions between refugees and hosts; potential for project implementation disruption
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Iterative conflict monitoring to assess ground situations and adapt implementation; engagement strategy with clear criteria for suspending activities or withdrawing from areas; extensive consultation with refugee and host communities; leveraging of peaceful coexistence and community committees; coordination with World Bank portfolio-wide engagement strategy

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: High (due to low capacity of government to enforce environmental compliance, dysfunctional legal and institutional arrangements, fragile context, and inability of World Bank to conduct on-ground supervision)
triggered_safeguard_policies: Environmental and Social Framework (ESF) - Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
Environmental and Social Framework - Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
Environmental and Social Framework - Labor and Working Conditions
Environmental and Social Framework - Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
Environmental and Social Framework - Community Health and Safety
Environmental and Social Framework - Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
Environmental and Social Framework - Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
Environmental and Social Framework - Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
Environmental and Social Framework - Cultural Heritage
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project will establish a Grievance Redress Mechanism (GRM) to provide project-affected parties with accessible and inclusive means to raise issues and grievances. GRM will be implemented by PMU and IOM through multiple channels including verbal reporting through community outreach assistants, anonymous notes in feedback boxes, text messages to hotline numbers. GRM will include survivor-centered protocols for GBV cases with appeals committees, confidentiality and non-discrimination measures. PMU will monitor implementation and ensure timely response. World Bank's corporate Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Inspection Panel also available.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant focus on gender equality and women's empowerment: minimum 50% female representation in BDCs/PDCs with 50% representation in leadership roles; separate women's prioritization process for subproject identification; women's economic empowerment through participation in construction works and O&M committees; gender transformative training for men and women; GBV prevention training; leadership and confidence building training for women; personal hygiene kits, breastfeeding spaces, and children's play areas during meetings; women's participation in O&M committees for sustainability; monitoring of women's benefits from employment opportunities; addressing women's limited access to income generation, public services, and heightened GBV risk
vulnerable_groups: Internally Displaced Persons (IDPs) - 2 million within South Sudan, 91% below poverty line
Refugees - 334,568 refugees and 4,243 asylum-seekers, primarily Sudanese in Maban and Pariang counties
Host communities - particularly in refugee-hosting areas facing resource constraints
Women and girls - facing disproportionate impacts from conflict, displacement, GBV, limited economic opportunities, and poor access to services
Youth - including refugee youth, facing marginalization and unrest; to be constructively engaged in planning, training, construction, and O&M
People with special needs and disabilities
Children - 54% of beneficiaries expected to be children

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project is designed with strong conflict-sensitive approach: continuous conflict analyses and just-in-time studies throughout implementation; conflict analysts on project team; conflict-sensitive implementation methodology; attention to contesting over housing, land, and property (HLP) to avoid building on contested land; ensuring IDPs, refugees, returnees, and host communities can all voice needs and benefit equitably; monitoring for negative impacts on local dynamics; inclusive and transparent planning to reduce tensions; investments in public goods to promote equity; fostering social cohesion through participatory planning and local conflict mitigation training; engagement with traditional authorities and customary institutions; support for community-based peacebuilding initiatives; iterative monitoring to identify and adjust for conflict risks
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: South Sudan ranks as one of least developed countries globally with decades of armed conflict since 1955. Renewed civil conflict December 2013-February 2020 with fragile peace and stalled implementation of key R-ARCSS provisions. High levels of inter-communal violence linked to national actors. 60% of population (7.2 million) in food insecurity crisis or worse. 4 million displaced (85% women and children), 2.3 million fled to neighboring countries. Protracted conflict has eroded social fabric and weakened formal and informal institutions. Customary law dominant but traditional authorities undermined by politicization and conflict. Low trust in government institutions. Weak social contract between civilians and state. Dependency on humanitarian assistance. Limited state presence and capacity. Competing claims over scarce resources (water, grazing land) exacerbated by climate-related disasters. Political economy implications of resource injection. Risk of violence over resource contestation.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: High security risk rating. Widespread and unpredictable violence including political, criminal, and ethnic violence. Inter-communal violence widespread. Project areas include some formerly controlled by warring parties. Insecurity limits accessibility to project sites. Project will develop strategy for implementation in insecure areas with objective criteria for classifying insecurity levels. Activities will be suspended if IOM cannot access areas. Option to replace counties if security deteriorates significantly. Minimum requirements for security risk assessments and incident reporting. Weekly security meetings, tracking of security alert status, site-specific emergency response protocols. Integration with UN security provisions (UNDSS). Non-militarized approach to local engagement with trust building as key. Communication and cooperation between stakeholders essential. Standard and emergency reporting systems throughout project cycle.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Project specifically targets refugee-hosting areas. South Sudan hosts 334,568 refugees and 4,243 asylum-seekers, 95% of whom are Sudanese hosted in Maban County (Upper Nile State) and Jamjang/Pariang (Ruweng Administrative Area). Project will scale up activities in two refugee-hosting counties (Pariang and Maban) to include larger numbers of refugees in infrastructure prioritization and benefits. US$20 million from IDA19 Window for Host Communities and Refugees (WHR) allocated to support refugees and host communities. Project aims to promote transition from humanitarian dependence to development-focused approach. Refugees have strong committee structures (peaceful coexistence, mother-to-mother, youth, agriculture, elderly and persons with disabilities committees) that will be leveraged for inclusion. UNHCR will provide guidance on refugee issues and support mobilization of refugee representatives. Equitable benefit allocation to both refugees and host communities. Project supports Government's Durable Solutions Strategy for Refugees, IDPs, Returnees, and Host Communities 2021-2025. Refugees granted freedom of movement and access to services under Refugee Act 2012. Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA) coordinates government policy. World Bank confirmed adequacy of South Sudan's refugee protection framework in consultation with UNHCR.
